title: Génoise
subtitle: The French foam sponge, leavened by egg alone
excerpt: A génoise is a whole-egg foam sponge: eggs and sugar are whisked over heat until the mixture is thick, pale, and tripled in volume, then flour and clarified butter are folded in without deflating the foam. The result is a tender, fine-crumbed sponge that forms the base of the classic layered French gâteaux.
sourceNotes: The génoise is a classic of the French pâtisserie repertoire, named after the city of Genoa where it originated and adopted by French professional baking in the 19th century. Escoffier documents the method in A Guide to Modern Cookery (1907), which entered the public domain. The whole-egg whisking method over a bain-marie is the defining technique and distinguishes it from the English sponge, which separates the eggs.

content.text: The génoise is a technique cake: it teaches how to build volume entirely from whisked eggs. There is no baking powder; the leavening is the air trapped in the foam. The skill is the fold: flour must go in without collapsing the structure, and clarified butter must go in at the end, when the foam is most vulnerable.
Melt the butter gently, skim the white foam from the surface, and pour the clear yellow liquid into a small bowl, leaving the milky solids behind. This is the clarified butter. Keep it warm.
Whisk the eggs over the bain-marie

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease a 20 cm round cake tin and line the base. Bring a pan of water to a gentle simmer. Put the eggs, sugar, and vanilla into a large heatproof bowl and set it over the pan, making sure the base of the bowl does not touch the water. Use the

technique: whisk continuously until the mixture is thick, very pale, and has roughly tripled in volume. This takes 8 to 10 minutes with a hand mixer. Remove from the heat and continue whisking for 2 more minutes until the bowl has cooled slightly.
Fold in the flour and butter

Sift the flour over the foam in three additions, folding gently with a large metal spoon or spatula after each addition. Use a figure-of-eight motion, turning the bowl and cutting through the centre. When the last traces of flour disappear, drizzle the warm clarified butter around the edge of the bowl and fold in quickly with no more than 10 strokes. Speed matters: overfolding collapses the foam. Pour immediately into the prepared tin.

Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the top is pale gold, the cake springs back to a gentle press, and a skewer comes out clean. Do not open the oven before 20 minutes. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn out carefully onto a rack to cool completely. Handle gently: the génoise is fragile.
